perturbation.original_text: 8. This Agreement shall be exclusively governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the state of Maryland, and the parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of that state for the resolution of any claim or matter arising under this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This Agreement shall be exclusively governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the state of California, and the parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of Maryland for the resolution of any claim or matter arising under this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California while maintaining jurisdiction in Maryland creates a conflict. California courts may not defer to Maryland courts based on a contract clause alone.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law and Forum Selection Conflicts
perturbation.law_explanation: While California law generally respects choice of law provisions, the exercise of jurisdiction ultimately rests with the court and the law applicable may be influenced by California public policy considerations, causing conflict when jurisdiction is contractually designated elsewhere.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The contract initially specified that Maryland law governs and Maryland courts have jurisdiction. The change designates California law as governing while keeping jurisdiction in Maryland. This creates a legal inconsistency. The Law Explanation suggests that California courts generally respect choice of law provisions but the application of law is ultimately subject to California public policy, leading to potential conflicts when jurisdiction is contractually set elsewhere. While snippet 1 (CCP 410.30) discusses forum and the court's ability to stay or dismiss an action in the interest of justice, it does not directly address the situation of conflicting governing law and jurisdiction clauses within a contract. The Perturbation Explanation states that California courts might not defer to Maryland courts based solely on a contract clause, highlighting the potential for conflicting legal interpretations and enforcement, thus creating a contradiction, and falling under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The Confidential Information shall at all times remain the property of Company, and Euler Hermes has no rights to the Confidentia l Information except as specified in this Agreement. Upon the Company’s written request, Euler Hermes shall promptly return to the Company or destroy the Confidential Information in its possession but may retain copies of any and all notes, analyses , references, or other material prepared by Euler Hermes that inc orporates any of the Confidential Information , which shall remain subject to the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement , nothwithstanding Paragraph 10 of this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Confidential Information shall at all times remain the property of Company, and Euler Hermes has no rights to the Confidentia l Information except as specified in this Agreement. Upon the Company’s written request, Euler Hermes shall return the Confidential Information in its possession in 90 days but may retain copies of any and all notes, analyses , references, or other material prepared by Euler Hermes that inc orporates any of the Confidential Information , which shall remain subject to the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement , nothwithstanding Paragraph 10 of this Agreement .
perturbation.explanation: Changing the deadline from 'promptly' to '90 days' for the return of confidential information upon request can be considered an unreasonably long delay.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secret Misappropriation - Statute of Limitations
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaying the return of confidential information for 90 days after a request could be seen as hindering the company's ability to promptly detect and address potential misappropriation, which can impact the statute of limitations for trade secret claims. The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) and state laws provide legal recourse for misappropriation, and prompt return of information is crucial for preserving legal options.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The change from 'promptly' to '90 days' for returning confidential information introduces a contradiction. The original contract ensures immediate action, crucial for mitigating potential harm from misappropriation. The modified contract introduces a delay that could hinder the company's ability to protect its trade secrets and enforce its rights under laws like the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as suggested by the Law Explanation. While the provided law snippet (18 USC 1836) primarily addresses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, the 'prompt' return of information supports the ability to pursue such proceedings effectively, which is undermined by the 90-day delay. Thus the change violates the requirement to ensure that trade secrets are protected without undue delay. The delay makes the contract inconsistent with protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall expire at the end of five (5) years from the date set forth below , and may be renewed upon mutual written consent of Euler Hermes and the Company .
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall expire at the end of twenty (20) years from the date set forth below , and may be renewed upon mutual written consent of Euler Hermes and the Company .
perturbation.explanation: Extending the agreement to 20 years could be deemed unreasonably long, especially if it restricts employee mobility or competitive activities. This can create issues under California law, which disfavors long-term restraints on trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A 20-year non-disclosure agreement, particularly if it covers broad categories of information, could be challenged as an unlawful restraint of trade because it limits the company's ability to use general knowledge and skills acquired during the term of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the agreement duration to 20 years, which, according to the perturbation explanation, could be deemed unreasonably long and create issues under California law. The law explanation suggests that a 20-year non-disclosure agreement could be challenged as an unlawful restraint of trade under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The extended duration could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint, directly contradicting the law's intention to prevent restrictions on lawful professions or trades. Therefore, the change introduces a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Euler Hermes acknowledges that monet ary damages for improper use or disclosure of Confidential Information may be inadequate. In the event of a breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, in addition to other available remedies, Company is entitled to seek equitable relief, including inj unctive relief, without the need to post a bond
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Euler Hermes acknowledges that monet ary damages for improper use or disclosure of Confidential Information may be inadequate. In the event of a breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, in addition to other available remedies, Company is entitled to seek equitable relief, including inj unctive relief, but must post a bond.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the waiver of bond requirement for injunctive relief contradicts general equitable principles. While the original agreement waived this right, removing the waiver and requiring a bond can be seen as conflicting with established legal precedent for injunctions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65(c) generally requires a bond for preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders, unless specifically waived by law. The original contract waived this requirement. Removing the waiver makes the injunction subject to the FRCP 65(c), which requires posting of a bond.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.justification: The original contract waived the bond requirement for injunctive relief. The changed text removes this waiver, creating an inconsistency with general equitable principles. The Law Explanation states that Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65(c) generally requires a bond for preliminary injunctions, unless specifically waived by law, and the original contract waived this requirement. By removing the waiver, the changed text reintroduces the bond requirement, contradicting the initial agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Rule 65, supports the general bond requirement for injunctions, and removing the waiver effectively reinstates this requirement where it had been previously eliminated. The perturbation directly reverses a previously waived obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 1. Euler Hermes shall keep the Confidential Information confidential and may disclose the Confidential Information only to its Affiliates , employees, contractors , or consultants for the Purpose described above and no other purpose. Euler Hermes shall be liable for any breach of confidentiality by any Affiliate, employee, contractor , or consultant.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Euler Hermes shall keep the Confidential Information confidential and may disclose the Confidential Information only to its Affiliates , employees, contractors , or consultants for the Purpose described above and no other purpose. Euler Hermes shall not be liable for any breach of confidentiality by any Affiliate, employee, contractor , or consultant unless the breach was intentional and directly authorized by Euler Hermes.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting liability for breaches of confidentiality to only intentional and directly authorized breaches significantly reduces the scope of protection for the disclosing party. This could be problematic under trade secret laws, which often impose liability for negligent or reckless disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), adopted by most states, provides for liability for misappropriation, which includes improper disclosure. Limiting liability to only intentional and authorized breaches conflicts with the UTSA, which also covers breaches resulting from negligence or other actions that fall short of intentional misconduct by the company itself. See §1(2)(ii)(B) of the UTSA. Even though Maryland is one of the states that has adopted UTSA, it is still possible to include this item because under UTSA, there are still standards of care in safeguarding the confidential information. The original agreement provides the higher standard of care without limits on liability.

perturbation.justification: The original contract imposes strict liability on Euler Hermes for any breach of confidentiality by its affiliates, employees, contractors, or consultants. The changed text limits this liability to only intentional and directly authorized breaches. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the UTSA, which also covers breaches resulting from negligence or actions that fall short of intentional misconduct. The original agreement provides a higher standard of care without limits on liability, whereas the modified version weakens this protection. The scraped snippets do not directly address the UTSA, but the Law Explanation describes how the modification conflicts with it. The modification creates a legal contradiction because it significantly reduces the scope of protection for the disclosing party, conflicting with the standards of care expected under trade secret laws like UTSA as described by the LLM.
